entries.title: Week 10 Reflection: The HRTO Got Its Reasoning Wrong — and the Court Said So
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Week 10 Reflection: The HRTO Got Its Reasoning Wrong — and the Court Said So
Admin Law in Daily Life · Week of March 17, 2026
This week in class:
Standard of review — choosing and applying. The
Vavilov
framework. What reasonableness actually demands from a decision-maker. When do courts intervene, and when do they defer?
On March 17 — the first class day of our standard of review week — the Ontario Divisional Court released
Burland v Precise ParkLink Inc
The Court quashed an HRTO decision that had dismissed a human rights application. The reason: the Tribunal’s decision was unreasonable under the
Vavilov
standard. Its reasons were “peremptory” — brief, conclusory, and devoid of the justification, transparency, and intelligibility that
Vavilov
demands.
The matter was sent back to the Tribunal to be decided again.
What
Vavilov
actually asks of decision-makers
Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov
restructured how Canadian courts review administrative decisions. The default is now a
presumption of reasonableness
— courts start by deferring to the decision-maker’s expertise and mandate.
But the presumption has teeth.
Vavilov
defined reasonableness not as “any defensible outcome” but as a decision that is:
Justified
: the decision-maker must explain
why
they reached the outcome, engaging with the key arguments and evidence
Transparent
: the reasoning must be visible and traceable, not hidden in a black box
Intelligible
: the logic must be coherent — the conclusion must follow from the reasoning
And the reasoning must be responsive to the
actual arguments made
by the parties. A decision that simply states a conclusion without engaging with the losing party’s case is not just thin — it may be unreasonable.
Why this matters for human rights adjudication

The HRTO handles some of the most important individual rights claims in Ontario. Dismissing an application — telling a person that their human rights complaint does not merit a hearing — is a significant exercise of public power.
When that dismissal comes with “peremptory reasons” that give the applicant no understanding of why their complaint failed, it does something more than fail the
Vavilov
standard. It fails the person.
Procedural fairness (which we studied in Week 8) includes the right to reasons.
Vavilov
makes the quality of those reasons a matter of
substantive reasonableness
. These two doctrines reinforce each other: a decision-maker who does not explain itself is both procedurally unfair and substantively unreasonable.
The bigger picture: deference with accountability
The standard of review framework is sometimes misread as courts staying out of administrative decisions. It is not.
Vavilov
is a framework for
structured deference
— deference calibrated to the quality of the decision-maker’s reasoning.
The more expert the decision-maker, the more their judgments on specialized questions should be respected. But the more they must
show their work
. Expertise without explanation is not enough.
Vavilov
made that explicit.
Watching the Divisional Court apply
Vavilov
to the HRTO in real time, this week of all weeks, felt like the course coming full circle: the law we have been studying all semester, working exactly as it is supposed to.
See also:
Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario
HRTO 2025 Case Law in Review — Ontario Bar Association

entries.body:
---
Week 10 Reflection: The HRTO Got Its Reasoning Wrong — and the Court Said So
Admin Law in Daily Life · Week of March 17, 2026
This week in class: Standard of review — choosing and applying. The Vavilov framework. What reasonableness actually demands from a decision-maker. When do courts intervene, and when do they defer?
---
On March 17 — the first class day of our standard of review week — the Ontario Divisional Court released Burland v Precise ParkLink Inc, 2026 ONSC 1587.
The Court quashed an HRTO decision that had dismissed a human rights application. The reason: the Tribunal’s decision was unreasonable under the Vavilov standard. Its reasons were “peremptory” — brief, conclusory, and devoid of the justification, transparency, and intelligibility that Vavilov demands.
The matter was sent back to the Tribunal to be decided again.
---
What Vavilov actually asks of decision-makers
Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 restructured how Canadian courts review administrative decisions. The default is now a presumption of reasonableness — courts start by deferring to the decision-maker’s expertise and mandate.
But the presumption has teeth. Vavilov defined reasonableness not as “any defensible outcome” but as a decision that is:
Justified: the decision-maker must explain why they reached the outcome, engaging with the key arguments and evidence
Transparent: the reasoning must be visible and traceable, not hidden in a black box
Intelligible: the logic must be coherent — the conclusion must follow from the reasoning
And the reasoning must be responsive to the actual arguments made by the parties. A decision that simply states a conclusion without engaging with the losing party’s case is not just thin — it may be unreasonable.
---
Why this matters for human rights adjudication
The HRTO handles some of the most important individual rights claims in Ontario. Dismissing an application — telling a person that their human rights complaint does not merit a hearing — is a significant exercise of public power.
When that dismissal comes with “peremptory reasons” that give the applicant no understanding of why their complaint failed, it does something more than fail the Vavilov standard. It fails the person.
Procedural fairness (which we studied in Week 8) includes the right to reasons. Vavilov makes the quality of those reasons a matter of substantive reasonableness. These two doctrines reinforce each other: a decision-maker who does not explain itself is both procedurally unfair and substantively unreasonable.
---
The bigger picture: deference with accountability
The standard of review framework is sometimes misread as courts staying out of administrative decisions. It is not. Vavilov is a framework for structured deference — deference calibrated to the quality of the decision-maker’s reasoning.
The more expert the decision-maker, the more their judgments on specialized questions should be respected. But the more they must show their work. Expertise without explanation is not enough. Vavilov made that explicit.
Watching the Divisional Court apply Vavilov to the HRTO in real time, this week of all weeks, felt like the course coming full circle: the law we have been studying all semester, working exactly as it is supposed to.
---
See also: Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 · Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario · HRTO 2025 Case Law in Review — Ontario Bar Association

entries.title: Week 9 Reflection: Zoom Court and the Apprehension of Bias
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law in Daily Life · Week of March 10, 2026
This week in class:
The rule against bias. Actual bias vs. reasonable apprehension of bias. The
Baker
para 46 test. Institutional bias, individual bias, and the conditions that make a fair hearing possible.

Most Ontario administrative hearings are now conducted over Zoom. The LTB, LAT, HRTO, and Consent and Capacity Board all moved to remote hearings during COVID and have largely stayed there. For many applicants, this has improved access — no travel, no childcare logistics, no time off work.
But I have been thinking this week about a different question:
what does Zoom do to the appearance of impartiality?
The
Baker
para 46 test
This week we studied the test for reasonable apprehension of bias from
Baker
para 46 (citing
Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board

The apprehension of bias must be a reasonable one, held by reasonable and right-minded persons, applying themselves to the question and obtaining thereon the required information… [T]he test is whether an informed person, viewing the matter realistically and practically — and having thought the matter through — would have a reasonable apprehension that the decision-maker would not decide fairly.
Note what the test does not require: proof of
actual
bias. It requires only a
reasonable apprehension
by a well-informed, realistic observer.
What Zoom makes harder to see

In a physical hearing room, the signals of engagement — or disengagement — are visible to everyone. An adjudicator who is looking out the window, or who is visibly impatient with one party’s submissions, or who leans forward when one side speaks and leans back when the other does, sends observable signals.

On Zoom, those signals are either amplified or hidden. An adjudicator with camera off is invisible. One who is multitasking may not be noticed. Pre-hearing written remarks in case management communications can surface in ways that might not have been recorded before.

And the parties themselves are at home, in their own spaces, separated from each other and from the decision-maker. The shared physical experience of a public hearing — which has always been part of what makes adjudication
legitimate
, not just technically fair — is absent.
The labour board deference case
Also this week, the Divisional Court issued a decision reinforcing that courts should defer to the
Ontario Labour Relations Board’s
findings on workplace disputes. The case involved a construction industry work assignment dispute — technical, expert-driven, squarely within the OLRB’s institutional competence.
But it reminded me of the flip side of the bias question: tribunals that are
expert

in their field are given deference precisely because* they know things courts don’t. The moment a party starts alleging bias without real evidence, they risk asking a court to substitute its own judgment for that expert judgment — which is not what judicial review is for.
The reasonable apprehension of bias test is calibrated to distinguish genuine fairness concerns from tactical attempts to relitigate outcomes.
What I’m watching

As administrative hearings become increasingly digital and informal, the procedural fairness frameworks we have built around in-person adjudication are going to be tested. The bias test — built on the idea of the
informed observer
watching the process — assumes there is something to observe.
Zoom hearings are good for access. Whether they are equally good for perceived legitimacy is a question I don’t think the doctrine has fully answered yet.
See also:
Baker v Canada, 1999 SCC 40, para 46
Ontario Labour Relations Board
Tribunals Ontario — Remote Hearings

entries.body:
---
Admin Law in Daily Life · Week of March 10, 2026
This week in class: The rule against bias. Actual bias vs. reasonable apprehension of bias. The Baker para 46 test. Institutional bias, individual bias, and the conditions that make a fair hearing possible.
---
Most Ontario administrative hearings are now conducted over Zoom. The LTB, LAT, HRTO, and Consent and Capacity Board all moved to remote hearings during COVID and have largely stayed there. For many applicants, this has improved access — no travel, no childcare logistics, no time off work.
But I have been thinking this week about a different question: what does Zoom do to the appearance of impartiality?
---
The Baker para 46 test
This week we studied the test for reasonable apprehension of bias from Baker para 46 (citing Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board [1978] 1 SCR 369):

---

On Zoom, those signals are either amplified or hidden. An adjudicator with camera off is invisible. One who is multitasking may not be noticed. Pre-hearing written remarks in case management communications can surface in ways that might not have been recorded before.
And the parties themselves are at home, in their own spaces, separated from each other and from the decision-maker. The shared physical experience of a public hearing — which has always been part of what makes adjudication legitimate, not just technically fair — is absent.
---
The labour board deference case
Also this week, the Divisional Court issued a decision reinforcing that courts should defer to the Ontario Labour Relations Board’s findings on workplace disputes. The case involved a construction industry work assignment dispute — technical, expert-driven, squarely within the OLRB’s institutional competence.
But it reminded me of the flip side of the bias question: tribunals that are expert in their field are given deference precisely because* they know things courts don’t. The moment a party starts alleging bias without real evidence, they risk asking a court to substitute its own judgment for that expert judgment — which is not what judicial review is for.

---

Zoom hearings are good for access. Whether they are equally good for perceived legitimacy is a question I don’t think the doctrine has fully answered yet.
---
See also: Baker v Canada, 1999 SCC 40, para 46 · Ontario Labour Relations Board · Tribunals Ontario — Remote Hearings

entries.title: Week 8: How Did We Get a Duty of Fairness, and What Does It Now Require?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of March 3, 2026

This week traced the evolution of procedural fairness in administrative law across more than 150 years of cases. The story is one of expansion: from a narrow common law principle limited to "judicial" functions, through a series of SCC decisions that dismantled the category-based approach and replaced it with a contextual interest-based standard, to
Baker v Canada
[1999] 2 SCR 817's five-factor synthesis, and finally to
Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz

(2022 SCC 29), which extended the fairness inquiry to the duration of proceedings. The central lesson was how the doctrine moved from asking "what type of function is this?" to asking "what is at stake for this person, and what process does that demand?"
Abrametz

is both course material and a concrete recent decision worth understanding precisely. Peter Abrametz was a Saskatchewan lawyer found guilty of conduct unbecoming, including making high-interest loans to vulnerable clients. Disciplinary proceedings commenced in 2012 and concluded in 2019 — a 71-month span. The Saskatchewan Court of Appeal stayed his disbarment, finding the delay constituted an abuse of process. The
SCC reversed 8-1
: Justice Rowe, writing for the majority, held that the delay was long but not
inordinate
given the complexity of the matter and Abrametz's own contribution to the timeline.
Administrative law commentators
noted that the significance of the case lies not in the outcome for Abrametz — he lost — but in the two-part test the majority articulated: delay constitutes an abuse of process where it is
inordinate
in all the circumstances,
and
where it causes
significant prejudice
to the person subject to it. Both elements must be present; the threshold is demanding.
Understanding why
Abrametz
looks the way it does requires following the evolutionary arc the week covered. The starting point is
Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works

(1863) 143 ER 414, where an English court held that even a statute silent on procedure did not eliminate the common law hearing right — "no man shall be condemned unheard." For a long time, courts confined this to "judicial" functions and refused to apply it to "administrative" ones, producing inconsistent results across provinces and decision-making bodies.
Ridge v Baldwin
[1964] AC 40 began the shift: Lord Reid held the right to a hearing depended on whether rights or interests were affected, not on whether the function was formally judicial. The SCC followed with
Nicholson v Haldimand-Norfolk Regional Police Commissioners
[1979] 1 SCR 311, which introduced a freestanding "duty of fairness" not dependent on classifying the function as quasi-judicial, and then
Martineau v Matsqui Institution
Cardinal v Director of Kent Institution
[1985] 2 SCR 643, which made it a general common law rule.
Knight v Indian Head School Division
[1990] 1 SCR 653 added that the
content
of the duty varies on a spectrum — minimal process for broad policy decisions affecting the public, more robust process for individualized decisions affecting named people's rights.
Baker

synthesized all of this into five factors: nature of the decision, statutory scheme, importance to the individual, legitimate expectations, and the agency's own procedural choices. The five factors do not produce a formula, but they give structure to the judgment. Applied to a serious AGCO licence revocation — a named individual, significant economic consequences, a statutory scheme that contemplates adjudication — the
Baker

factors would require meaningful notice, an opportunity to respond, and written reasons. Applied to a broad IRCC policy announcement affecting all international students in a given category, the duty is much thinner.
Abrametz

extended the inquiry further: it is not only the content of the hearing steps that counts, but the time in which they unfold. Delay that is inordinate and causes significant prejudice — reputational harm, professional uncertainty, prolonged anxiety — is itself a fairness violation. For international students navigating immigration proceedings that can span years, this matters. An immigration appeal, a refugee claim, or a permit refusal under judicial review can impose the same kinds of prejudice that Abrametz suffered: precarious status, inability to plan, restricted rights.
Abrametz

confirms that delay is a cognizable legal wrong, not just an administrative inconvenience — though the threshold of inordinate delay plus significant prejudice is high enough that most delays will not cross it. What the case establishes, at minimum, is that duration is a dimension of fairness the doctrine now formally recognizes.

Cases referenced: Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz, 2022 SCC 29 | Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works (1863) 143 ER 414 | Ridge v Baldwin, [1964] AC 40 | Nicholson v Haldimand-Norfolk Regional Police Commissioners, [1979] 1 SCR 311 | Martineau v Matsqui Institution, [1980] 1 SCR 602 | Cardinal v Director of Kent Institution, [1985] 2 SCR 643 | Knight v Indian Head School Division, [1990] 1 SCR 653 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817
Sources
Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz, 2022 SCC 29 (Supreme Court of Canada)
Analysis of Abrametz: Procedural Fairness, Undue Delay and Remedial Flexibility (Administrative Law Matters)
Supreme Court revisits issue of delay in administrative proceedings (HRLSC)
Delayed Disciplinary Proceedings Not an Abuse of Process, SCC Rules (MLT Aikins)

entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of March 3, 2026
This week traced the evolution of procedural fairness in administrative law across more than 150 years of cases. The story is one of expansion: from a narrow common law principle limited to "judicial" functions, through a series of SCC decisions that dismantled the category-based approach and replaced it with a contextual interest-based standard, to Baker v Canada [1999] 2 SCR 817's five-factor synthesis, and finally to Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz (2022 SCC 29), which extended the fairness inquiry to the duration of proceedings. The central lesson was how the doctrine moved from asking "what type of function is this?" to asking "what is at stake for this person, and what process does that demand?"
Abrametz is both course material and a concrete recent decision worth understanding precisely. Peter Abrametz was a Saskatchewan lawyer found guilty of conduct unbecoming, including making high-interest loans to vulnerable clients. Disciplinary proceedings commenced in 2012 and concluded in 2019 — a 71-month span. The Saskatchewan Court of Appeal stayed his disbarment, finding the delay constituted an abuse of process. The SCC reversed 8-1: Justice Rowe, writing for the majority, held that the delay was long but not inordinate given the complexity of the matter and Abrametz's own contribution to the timeline. Administrative law commentators noted that the significance of the case lies not in the outcome for Abrametz — he lost — but in the two-part test the majority articulated: delay constitutes an abuse of process where it is inordinate in all the circumstances, and where it causes significant prejudice to the person subject to it. Understanding why Abrametz looks the way it does requires following the evolutionary arc the week covered. The starting point is Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works (1863) 143 ER 414, where an English court held that even a statute silent on procedure did not eliminate the common law hearing right — "no man shall be condemned unheard." For a long time, courts confined this to "judicial" functions and refused to apply it to "administrative" ones, producing inconsistent results across provinces and decision-making bodies. The five factors do not produce a formula, but they give structure to the judgment. ---
Sources

entries.title: Week 7: Legitimate Expectations and the PGWP Policy Shift
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of February 24, 2026

This week covered the doctrine of legitimate expectations — the principle that, in certain circumstances, a person who relies on a clear government representation has a legal entitlement to procedural protection before that representation is resiled from. Canadian law draws a firm line: legitimate expectations is a
procedural

doctrine only. A successful claim gives the applicant a right to a fair process — notice, an opportunity to be heard — before a policy changes. It does not guarantee any substantive outcome. The three central cases were
Mavi v Canada
(2011 SCC 30), which tied the doctrine to formal undertakings and confirmed the procedural-only limit;
Agraira v Canada
(2013 SCC 36), which extended it to published ministerial guidelines where sufficiently clear and consistent; and
Baker v Canada

[1999] 2 SCR 817, which emphasized that context and the degree of individual reliance matter in determining what procedural protection is owed. The doctrine's threshold — a representation that is clear, unambiguous, and unqualified — was the central point of discussion.
On September 18, 2024, IRCC
announced significant changes to Post-Graduation Work Permit eligibility
, introducing a new requirement that PGWP applicants' programs align with
specific labour market fields

— STEM, healthcare, agriculture, trades, and transportation. Hospitality, social services, and the arts were excluded. The transition protection was narrow: students who had applied for their initial study permit before November 1, 2024 were exempt; those already enrolled but who had not yet submitted that application were not.
As Green and Spiegel summarized
, students who had chosen specific programs precisely because IRCC's materials indicated those programs would qualify for a PGWP found themselves subject to rules they had no way to anticipate.
Protests followed across Canada

Applying the week's doctrine to these facts produces a concrete answer, even if it is not a satisfying one. The legitimate expectations analysis starts with the threshold requirement: was IRCC's published PGWP guidance a representation that was clear, unambiguous, and unqualified? The answer is almost certainly no. IRCC's web publications on PGWP eligibility have consistently included qualifications — rules are subject to change, programs must continue to meet requirements, no binding commitment is made. Under
Mavi
, the doctrine requires something closer to a formal undertaking made to an identified party with specific legal content. A general eligibility page with caveats does not meet that standard. Under
Agraira

, a published guideline might generate expectations if it is sufficiently clear and consistently applied — but the caveats in IRCC's publications were precisely designed to preserve the government's flexibility to change the rules, which takes the PGWP materials below the
Agraira
threshold.

Even if the threshold were met, the procedural-only limit would significantly constrain what students could obtain. A successful legitimate expectations claim would have required IRCC to give affected students notice of the proposed change and a meaningful opportunity to comment before it took effect — not a right to have the old rules applied to their applications. If IRCC had conducted that process and then still adopted the new field-of-study requirement, the legal outcome for students would be identical. The doctrine gives access to process, not to a result.

The honest conclusion is that the legitimate expectations doctrine, as currently defined in Canadian law, provides limited protection against the kind of harm the 2024 PGWP changes caused. Students who structured significant financial and immigration decisions around government-published eligibility information received no legally enforceable procedural protection when those rules changed. The gap between the harm experienced and the legal remedy available reflects a deliberate doctrinal choice — to preserve government flexibility to change policy — rather than an oversight. Whether that balance is correctly struck is a policy question the courts have consistently declined to revisit.

Cases referenced: Mavi v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2011 SCC 30 | Agraira v Canada (Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness), 2013 SCC 36 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817
Sources
IRCC announces tighter cap for study permits and changes to PGWP eligibility (CIC News, Sept 2024)
Post-graduation work permit: Field of study requirement (
Canada.ca
What You Need to Know About the New PGWP Requirements (Green and Spiegel)
Indian students protest PGWP rules in Canada (The PIE News)

entries.title: Week 5: Did the LTB Actually Survive s. 96, or Just Pass a Low Bar?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of February 10, 2026

This week covered the constitutional constraints on provincial delegation to administrative tribunals under s. 96, using the Landlord and Tenant Board as the central example. The analytical framework is the three-step test from the
Reference re Residential Tenancies Act, 1979

(Ontario) [1981] 1 SCR 714: characterize the power delegated; ask whether it broadly conforms to jurisdiction held exclusively or primarily by superior courts at Confederation; and if so, determine whether the delegation is part of a broader administrative scheme that justifies specialization. The 1981 Ontario RTA scheme failed — the tribunal's eviction and possession powers too closely resembled historic superior court jurisdiction, and the surrounding administrative scheme was not substantial enough to save it. The legislature redesigned around the
Residential Tenancies Act, 2006
, and the modern LTB survived s. 96 scrutiny. The
Reference re Residential Tenancies Act
(Nova Scotia) [1996] 1 SCR 186 confirmed the same approach for a comparable provincial scheme. This week's question was what that constitutional survival actually guarantees.
In May 2023, Ontario's Ombudsman released
Administrative Justice Delayed, Fairness Denied

, a three-year investigation into the LTB. The report documented a backlog of more than 38,000 cases and median wait times of seven to eight months for landlord applications — with some tenant matters taking up to two years.
CBC News described the LTB as "fundamentally failing."
The
Advocacy Centre for Tenants Ontario
called the digital-first hearing model a failed experiment.
Tribunal Watch Ontario
published ongoing statistics. The scale of this failure raises a precise question: what did the constitutional analysis from class actually say about whether this could happen?
The answer is that the three-step test was never designed to prevent it. The test asks whether the
type

of power can be constitutionally delegated to the LTB. It does not address how well that power must be exercised, how many adjudicators must be employed, or how long hearings may take. The LTB's 2023 backlog was an operational failure, not a constitutional one. The modern LTB cleared all three steps of the analysis — it exercises a distinct statutory scheme, not merely codified common law, and the scheme is sufficiently developed to justify specialization. Constitutional validity and operational adequacy are separate standards, and the s. 96 framework speaks only to the first.
Crevier v AG Quebec
MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson
[1995] 4 SCR 725 provide the structural brackets that contain the LTB's operation.
Crevier

ensures that if the LTB exceeds its jurisdiction — decides something it has no statutory authority to decide — superior courts retain the power to correct that excess, regardless of any statutory language purporting to limit review.
MacMillan Bloedel

ensures that superior court oversight cannot be fully abolished. Both cases constrain what the legislature can take away from the courts; neither constrains what resources the legislature must give to the tribunal. A tenant who waits eight months for a hearing on a habitability complaint has been given access to a constitutionally valid tribunal operating within the
Crevier
MacMillan Bloedel
limits. The constitutional framework cannot tell them when their hearing will be scheduled.

For international students in Ontario — who disproportionately rent, often in housing where maintenance issues arise, and who may be less aware of how to navigate LTB procedures — the gap between formal constitutional validity and functional adequacy is significant. The constitutional analysis was worth doing: it explains why the LTB exists, why it survived the 1981 challenge, and what judicial oversight remains available. But understanding those limits is also what makes it clear that the Ombudsman's report was documenting something the constitutional framework was never equipped to prevent.

Cases referenced: Reference re Residential Tenancies Act, 1979 (Ontario), [1981] 1 SCR 714 | Reference re Residential Tenancies Act (Nova Scotia), [1996] 1 SCR 186 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220 | MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725
Sources
Administrative Justice Delayed, Fairness Denied — Ombudsman Ontario (May 2023)
Ontario's Landlord and Tenant Board 'fundamentally failing,' ombudsman finds (CBC News)
The Landlord and Tenant Board is broken (Advocacy Centre for Tenants Ontario)
LTB Statement of Concern: The Numbers Speak for Themselves (Tribunal Watch Ontario)

entries.title: Week 4: When Does a Guideline Become Illegal Control?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of February 3, 2026

This week covered soft law — policies, guidelines, and internal instructions that agencies use to structure discretion without formal rule-making — and introduced the fettering doctrine that governs when those tools cross a legal line. The key tension runs between two cases.
Maple Lodge Farms Ltd v Canada

[1982] 2 SCR 2 established that mechanically applying a policy without genuine consideration of the individual case is not a lawful exercise of discretion — it is a substitution of the policy for the judgment Parliament intended. But
Baker v Canada
[1999] 2 SCR 817 treated ministerial guidelines as legitimate and important context for structuring discretion, and
Agraira v Canada

(2013 SCC 36) confirmed that a reasonable officer would take published guidelines seriously. The doctrinal message is that guidelines are necessary and lawful as a framework, but cannot function as a checklist that replaces case-by-case judgment.

That tension maps directly onto something immigration lawyers have raised publicly for years. In 2022 and 2023, concerns were documented about how IRCC officers assessed the "genuineness of studies" requirement for study permit applications. The operative criteria come from internal
Program Delivery Instructions
— documents that are not published as regulations and not subject to parliamentary oversight. The
International Bar Association has noted
that "genuineness" refusals are among the most common and hardest to challenge, often arriving as boilerplate with no applicant-specific reasoning. Lawyers have used
access-to-information requests and procedural fairness letter processes

to reconstruct which criteria were actually applied — sometimes finding that officers appeared to be working through a checklist of indicators (travel history, financial ties, family circumstances) rather than engaging with the individual file.
This is the
Maple Lodge Farms
problem applied to immigration. The PDI provides a structured framework; the officer works through it; a refusal is issued. The legal question is whether the officer used the framework as a
tool for judgment
or as a
substitute for judgment

. A refusal that identifies that the applicant "has not demonstrated sufficient ties to their home country" without connecting that finding to any specific evidence in the file looks more like the latter. The challenge, which the cases do not fully resolve, is that from the outside a thoughtful application of the framework and a mechanical one can produce identical output. Both cite the same criteria; both arrive at the same result. The fettering problem is partly an evidentiary one.
Vavilov
(2019 SCC 65) is where the doctrine provides a practical mechanism. Reasonableness review under
Vavilov

requires decision-makers to produce reasons that actually grapple with the key considerations in the record — including a demonstration that the guidelines were applied to the specific applicant's circumstances, not just cited. A refusal letter that reproduces PDI language without showing how the officer assessed the particular evidence is more vulnerable to a finding of unreasonableness than one that engages with the file.
Vavilov
does not eliminate fettering, but it makes it harder to sustain on review by requiring visible reasoning.

The gap that remains is upstream of the individual decision. The PDIs themselves are not easily accessible to applicants. If you don't know the operative framework, you cannot structure your application to address its criteria, and you cannot identify on review whether those criteria were applied thoughtfully. The fettering doctrine addresses the quality of the decision made; the opacity of the framework that shaped it is a separate problem — one that
Vavilov
and
Baker
together do not fully answer.

Cases referenced: Maple Lodge Farms Ltd v Canada, [1982] 2 SCR 2 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817 | Agraira v Canada (Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness), 2013 SCC 36 | Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
Sources
Study permit refusals in Canada (International Bar Association)
IRCC Procedural Fairness Letter — How to Respond (Canadian Currents Immigration)
Study Permit Application Refusals (Canadavisa)
IRCC Ministerial Instructions (
Canada.ca

entries.title: Week 3: Does Institutional Design Actually Matter?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of January 27, 2026
This week the course covered the constitutional framework governing how administrative tribunals are designed. Three cases define the boundaries.
Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v BC

(2001 SCC 52) held that structural features like member tenure and independence are not constitutionally required — they are legislative choices, and courts will not impose their own design preferences on Parliament or the provinces.
Crevier v AG Quebec
[1981] 2 SCR 220 set the constitutional floor: whatever the legislature designs, it cannot immunize a tribunal's jurisdictional errors from superior court review.
MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson

[1995] 4 SCR 725 established the outer hard limit: the irreducible core of superior court jurisdiction cannot be fully abolished. Together, these three cases map a spectrum of legislative freedom — wide design latitude under
Ocean Port
, a jurisdictional floor under
Crevier
, and an outer constitutional stop under
MacMillan Bloedel
In December 2021, the AGCO
suspended and moved to revoke the liquor licence of Zucchinis Cucina
, an Oakville restaurant that had admitted unvaccinated dine-in patrons during Ontario's proof-of-vaccination period. It was part of a broader enforcement campaign — the AGCO's
COVID-19 pandemic response

describes Compliance Officials participating in multi-ministry enforcement sweeps. Some operators complained about the speed of the actions and argued that the AGCO's published progressive enforcement framework was not being consistently followed. The right way to assess those complaints is to run them through the week's constitutional framework.
Start with
Ocean Port
. The AGCO's structure — a governing Commission setting policy, a Registrar applying it to individual cases, and a separate Licence Appeal Tribunal reviewing challenges — is entirely a product of the
Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario Act, 2019
. Under
Ocean Port

, all of that is a valid legislative choice. The degree of Commission oversight over the Registrar, the partial separation between policy-setting and adjudication, the tenure conditions of LAT members — these are design decisions the legislature was entitled to make, and a court applying
Ocean Port
will not second-guess them. An operator who thinks the AGCO is structurally biased has no constitutional argument based on design alone.
Next,
Crevier
. The jurisdictional floor matters if the Registrar's enforcement exceeded its statutory authority — for example, if it suspended a licence on grounds that the
AGCO Act

does not actually authorize. If the Registrar acted outside its statutory remit, the LAT and, beyond it, superior courts retain jurisdiction to review that excess, and no privative clause can take that away. On the COVID enforcement facts, the statutory authority question turns on whether violations of public health orders issued under separate legislation were valid grounds for AGCO action under the
AGCO Act
. That is a genuine legal question, but the AGCO does not appear to have faced a successful jurisdictional challenge on this basis, suggesting courts found the statutory authority was present.
The conclusion from applying all three cases to the Zucchinis Cucina scenario is that the AGCO's design and its COVID enforcement actions were constitutionally sound.
Ocean Port
protected the design;
Crevier
preserved judicial oversight;
MacMillan Bloedel
's outer limit was nowhere near triggered. But the more important takeaway is that constitutional validity does not exhaust the legal inquiry.
Law Society of Ontario v AA
(2026 ONCA 47), decided just recently, shows that the ONCA will separately ask whether the
process
in a specific case was adequate to the stakes involved — a
Baker

-style procedural fairness question that operates entirely independently of whether the tribunal's structure is constitutionally valid. For AGCO operators who received little notice before a suspension, that procedural fairness question is the relevant one, and it is where the real legal dispute lives.

Cases referenced: Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v British Columbia, 2001 SCC 52 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220 | MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725 | Law Society of Ontario v AA, 2026 ONCA 47
Sources
AGCO suspends and moves to revoke the liquor licence of Zucchinis Cucina (AGCO)
The AGCO's response to the COVID-19 pandemic (AGCO)

entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of January 27, 2026
This week the course covered the constitutional framework governing how administrative tribunals are designed. Three cases define the boundaries. Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v BC (2001 SCC 52) held that structural features like member tenure and independence are not constitutionally required — they are legislative choices, and courts will not impose their own design preferences on Parliament or the provinces. Crevier v AG Quebec [1981] 2 SCR 220 set the constitutional floor: whatever the legislature designs, it cannot immunize a tribunal's jurisdictional errors from superior court review. MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson [1995] 4 SCR 725 established the outer hard limit: the irreducible core of superior court jurisdiction cannot be fully abolished. Together, these three cases map a spectrum of legislative freedom — wide design latitude under Ocean Port, a jurisdictional floor under Crevier, and an outer constitutional stop under MacMillan Bloedel.
In December 2021, the AGCO suspended and moved to revoke the liquor licence of Zucchinis Cucina, an Oakville restaurant that had admitted unvaccinated dine-in patrons during Ontario's proof-of-vaccination period. It was part of a broader enforcement campaign — the AGCO's COVID-19 pandemic response describes Compliance Officials participating in multi-ministry enforcement sweeps. Some operators complained about the speed of the actions and argued that the AGCO's published progressive enforcement framework was not being consistently followed. The right way to assess those complaints is to run them through the week's constitutional framework.
Start with Ocean Port. The AGCO's structure — a governing Commission setting policy, a Registrar applying it to individual cases, and a separate Licence Appeal Tribunal reviewing challenges — is entirely a product of the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario Act, 2019. Under Ocean Port, all of that is a valid legislative choice. The degree of Commission oversight over the Registrar, the partial separation between policy-setting and adjudication, the tenure conditions of LAT members — these are design decisions the legislature was entitled to make, and a court applying Ocean Port will not second-guess them. An operator who thinks the AGCO is structurally biased has no constitutional argument based on design alone.
Next, Crevier. The jurisdictional floor matters if the Registrar's enforcement exceeded its statutory authority — for example, if it suspended a licence on grounds that the AGCO Act does not actually authorize. If the Registrar acted outside its statutory remit, the LAT and, beyond it, superior courts retain jurisdiction to review that excess, and no privative clause can take that away. On the COVID enforcement facts, the statutory authority question turns on whether violations of public health orders issued under separate legislation were valid grounds for AGCO action under the AGCO Act. That is a genuine legal question, but the AGCO does not appear to have faced a successful jurisdictional challenge on this basis, suggesting courts found the statutory authority was present.
The conclusion from applying all three cases to the Zucchinis Cucina scenario is that the AGCO's design and its COVID enforcement actions were constitutionally sound. Ocean Port protected the design; Crevier preserved judicial oversight; MacMillan Bloedel's outer limit was nowhere near triggered. But the more important takeaway is that constitutional validity does not exhaust the legal inquiry. Law Society of Ontario v AA (2026 ONCA 47), decided just recently, shows that the ONCA will separately ask whether the process in a specific case was adequate to the stakes involved — a Baker-style procedural fairness question that operates entirely independently of whether the tribunal's structure is constitutionally valid. For AGCO operators who received little notice before a suspension, that procedural fairness question is the relevant one, and it is where the real legal dispute lives.
Cases referenced: Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v British Columbia, 2001 SCC 52 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220 | MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725 | Law Society of Ontario v AA, 2026 ONCA 47
---
Sources
AGCO suspends and moves to revoke the liquor licence of Zucchinis Cucina (AGCO)
The AGCO's response to the COVID-19 pandemic (AGCO)

entries.title: Week 2: What Makes a Decision "Governmental Enough" to Review?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Week 2 Reflection: What Makes a Decision "Governmental Enough" to Review?
Admin Law | Week of January 20, 2026

This week moved from identifying administrative decision-makers to a sharper question: among bodies that exercise power under legislation, which ones are "governmental" enough for their decisions to be challenged by judicial review? The
Wall

test (2018 SCC 26) is the current answer: judicial review lies where the decision-maker exercises public power — authority that derives from legislation in a way that makes the function governmental in character. Where authority comes from contract or consent, private law remedies apply instead. Before
Wall
, three competing lines of cases produced inconsistent results — the
Lindenburger
source-of-power line, the
Air Canada v Toronto Port Authority
multi-factor governmental-function line, and the
McCaw/Lakeside Colony
distinction between statutory powers and private powers under a regulatory scheme.
Wall
tried to replace all three with a single, cleaner question. The issue is whether that simplification works in practice.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, Ontario condominium corporations issued rules restricting common area access, guest policies, and elevator use for residents believed to be infectious.
CBC News reported
on a downtown Toronto condo requiring proof of vaccination to use amenities; the
Globe and Mail noted

that boards were largely left to navigate the rules without provincial guidance. Some residents who faced fines wanted to know whether they could challenge these decisions through judicial review. The
Wall
framework gives a clear answer: no. A condominium corporation's authority comes from the
Condominium Act

, but the nature of the relationship between a condo corporation and its unit owners is fundamentally private — it resembles a community of property owners managing shared space, not a government regulating the public.
Courts upheld several COVID condo policies
through the
Condominium Act
's own dispute mechanisms and the Condominium Authority Tribunal, confirming that private law, not judicial review, is the right channel.
The
Wall
result is probably correct for condo boards. But running the pre-
Wall
lines against the same facts reveals why
Wall
was needed and why it still creates difficulty at the margins. The
Lindenburger
source-of-power line would have noted that a condo board's authority traces to the
Condominium Act
— a statute — and might have pointed toward reviewability. The
Air Canada TPA
multi-factor line, looking at government funding and control, would have landed on "not sufficiently governmental." The
McCaw
line would have asked whether the board was exercising a statutory power and might have reached a third result. The inconsistency across frameworks for the same body is exactly the problem
Wall
addressed. Its single "public power" question produces a cleaner outcome: the function is private in character, so judicial review is unavailable.
The harder problem is
Aga v Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church of Canada St. Mary Cathedral
(2021 SCC 22), decided three years after
Wall
. The SCC held that a religious community's decision to expel members was not judicially reviewable. The
Wall

logic applied cleanly: the church's authority is consensual, not governmental. But the case highlights that powerful private institutions can make life-altering decisions — professional, social, financial — with no public law check whatsoever. For me as an international student, the most consequential decision-making body in my daily life is not a government agency but my university. Universities are created by provincial statute, receive public funding, and make discipline decisions that can affect study permit status and immigration outcomes. Whether university disciplinary proceedings are subject to judicial review turns on exactly the
Wall
characterization, and
MacKinnon v University of Guelph
(2025 FC 422) shows courts are still actively working this out.
Wall
simplified the test; it did not make the boundary cases easier to call.

Cases referenced: Wall v Independent Planning Association, 2018 SCC 26 | Aga v Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church of Canada St. Mary Cathedral, 2021 SCC 22 | MacKinnon v University of Guelph, 2025 FC 422
Sources
Toronto condo building brings in vaccine policy for amenities (CBC News)
In condo land, COVID-19 vaccine rules are hazy (The Globe and Mail)
Condo COVID-19 Policy Upheld as Enforceable by Court (Lash Condo Law)

entries.body:
Week 2 Reflection: What Makes a Decision "Governmental Enough" to Review?
Admin Law | Week of January 20, 2026
This week moved from identifying administrative decision-makers to a sharper question: among bodies that exercise power under legislation, which ones are "governmental" enough for their decisions to be challenged by judicial review? The Wall test (2018 SCC 26) is the current answer: judicial review lies where the decision-maker exercises public power — authority that derives from legislation in a way that makes the function governmental in character. Where authority comes from contract or consent, private law remedies apply instead. Before Wall, three competing lines of cases produced inconsistent results — the Lindenburger source-of-power line, the Air Canada v Toronto Port Authority multi-factor governmental-function line, and the McCaw/Lakeside Colony distinction between statutory powers and private powers under a regulatory scheme. Wall tried to replace all three with a single, cleaner question. The issue is whether that simplification works in practice.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, Ontario condominium corporations issued rules restricting common area access, guest policies, and elevator use for residents believed to be infectious. CBC News reported on a downtown Toronto condo requiring proof of vaccination to use amenities; the Globe and Mail noted that boards were largely left to navigate the rules without provincial guidance. Some residents who faced fines wanted to know whether they could challenge these decisions through judicial review. The Wall framework gives a clear answer: no. A condominium corporation's authority comes from the Condominium Act, but the nature of the relationship between a condo corporation and its unit owners is fundamentally private — it resembles a community of property owners managing shared space, not a government regulating the public. Courts upheld several COVID condo policies through the Condominium Act's own dispute mechanisms and the Condominium Authority Tribunal, confirming that private law, not judicial review, is the right channel.
The Wall result is probably correct for condo boards. But running the pre-Wall lines against the same facts reveals why Wall was needed and why it still creates difficulty at the margins. The Lindenburger source-of-power line would have noted that a condo board's authority traces to the Condominium Act — a statute — and might have pointed toward reviewability. The Air Canada TPA multi-factor line, looking at government funding and control, would have landed on "not sufficiently governmental." The McCaw line would have asked whether the board was exercising a statutory power and might have reached a third result. The inconsistency across frameworks for the same body is exactly the problem Wall addressed. Its single "public power" question produces a cleaner outcome: the function is private in character, so judicial review is unavailable.
The harder problem is Aga v Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church of Canada St. Mary Cathedral (2021 SCC 22), decided three years after Wall. The SCC held that a religious community's decision to expel members was not judicially reviewable. The Wall logic applied cleanly: the church's authority is consensual, not governmental. But the case highlights that powerful private institutions can make life-altering decisions — professional, social, financial — with no public law check whatsoever. For me as an international student, the most consequential decision-making body in my daily life is not a government agency but my university. Universities are created by provincial statute, receive public funding, and make discipline decisions that can affect study permit status and immigration outcomes. Whether university disciplinary proceedings are subject to judicial review turns on exactly the Wall characterization, and MacKinnon v University of Guelph (2025 FC 422) shows courts are still actively working this out. Wall simplified the test; it did not make the boundary cases easier to call.
Cases referenced: Wall v Independent Planning Association, 2018 SCC 26 | Aga v Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church of Canada St. Mary Cathedral, 2021 SCC 22 | MacKinnon v University of Guelph, 2025 FC 422
---
Sources
Toronto condo building brings in vaccine policy for amenities (CBC News)
In condo land, COVID-19 vaccine rules are hazy (The Globe and Mail)
Condo COVID-19 Policy Upheld as Enforceable by Court (Lash Condo Law)

entries.title: Week 1: Who Is Actually Making the Decision?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of January 13, 2026

This week introduced the foundational question of administrative law: what makes a body an "administrative decision-maker" subject to judicial review? The answer the course gave is the source-of-power test — a body exercises public law power when its authority derives from statute, and it is that statutory delegation that triggers administrative law accountability.
Roncarelli v Duplessis

[1959] SCR 121 is the classic illustration: when a government official used statutory power to revoke a liquor licence as personal retaliation, the SCC held that the exercise of that authority was legally constrained precisely because the power was public. Statutory source equals public accountability.
When I renewed my study permit last year, the first step was not submitting anything to IRCC — it was booking an appointment through
VFS Global

, a private company contracted by the government to collect biometrics and application documents. You pay a separate service fee to VFS, attend their commercial office, and only then does IRCC receive your materials and make the actual decision. IRCC's own
VFS consent form

states explicitly: "VFS Global is not an agent of the Government of Canada but is a completely independent entity operating under contract." That sentence matters legally. If something goes wrong at the VFS stage — documents mishandled, biometrics incorrectly recorded — the source-of-power test points to a clear answer: VFS has no statutory authority, it is not an administrative decision-maker, and I have no administrative law remedy against it.
A similar pattern appears with ServiceOntario, which has contracted out vehicle and driver licensing services to private registry agents — pharmacies, private offices, dealerships — for years. The
Auditor General of Ontario

has flagged the accountability gap this creates: when private operators make errors affecting people's licences, there is no clear public law path to correction because the operators are not statutory decision-makers. These are not hypothetical problems. They affect real people whose licences, permits, and status depend on functions that the government has delegated to private hands.

Applying the source-of-power test to VFS produces a technically correct but practically unsatisfying result. VFS receives documents; IRCC makes the eligibility decision. The statutory power to grant or refuse a study permit lives with IRCC under the
Immigration and Refugee Protection Act

. VFS exercises no delegated public law power and is therefore, on this analysis, outside administrative law's reach. What this conclusion misses is that VFS is a mandatory gateway to a statutory right — there is no way to submit a study permit application without going through VFS for biometrics. An error at that stage can delay or derail an application with serious downstream consequences for immigration status, employment authorization, and study continuity. The person harmed has no direct administrative law remedy against the body that caused the harm.
The source-of-power test was developed when government acted through its own officials, not through contracted private infrastructure. The
Roncarelli

principle — that public authority creates public accountability — has not yet been applied to ask who is accountable when public authority is intentionally routed through private architecture. Until the doctrine catches up with the reality of outsourced service delivery, there is a genuine accountability gap that affects people who have no way to avoid it.
Cases referenced: Roncarelli v Duplessis, [1959] SCR 121
Sources
Biometrics — Where to give your fingerprints and photo (IRCC)
VFS Global Consent Form for Canadian Visa Applications
Office of the Auditor General of Ontario

entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of January 13, 2026
This week introduced the foundational question of administrative law: what makes a body an "administrative decision-maker" subject to judicial review? The answer the course gave is the source-of-power test — a body exercises public law power when its authority derives from statute, and it is that statutory delegation that triggers administrative law accountability. Roncarelli v Duplessis [1959] SCR 121 is the classic illustration: when a government official used statutory power to revoke a liquor licence as personal retaliation, the SCC held that the exercise of that authority was legally constrained precisely because the power was public. Statutory source equals public accountability.
When I renewed my study permit last year, the first step was not submitting anything to IRCC — it was booking an appointment through VFS Global, a private company contracted by the government to collect biometrics and application documents. You pay a separate service fee to VFS, attend their commercial office, and only then does IRCC receive your materials and make the actual decision. IRCC's own VFS consent form states explicitly: "VFS Global is not an agent of the Government of Canada but is a completely independent entity operating under contract." That sentence matters legally. If something goes wrong at the VFS stage — documents mishandled, biometrics incorrectly recorded — the source-of-power test points to a clear answer: VFS has no statutory authority, it is not an administrative decision-maker, and I have no administrative law remedy against it.
A similar pattern appears with ServiceOntario, which has contracted out vehicle and driver licensing services to private registry agents — pharmacies, private offices, dealerships — for years. The Auditor General of Ontario has flagged the accountability gap this creates: when private operators make errors affecting people's licences, there is no clear public law path to correction because the operators are not statutory decision-makers. These are not hypothetical problems. They affect real people whose licences, permits, and status depend on functions that the government has delegated to private hands.
Applying the source-of-power test to VFS produces a technically correct but practically unsatisfying result. VFS receives documents; IRCC makes the eligibility decision. The statutory power to grant or refuse a study permit lives with IRCC under the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act. VFS exercises no delegated public law power and is therefore, on this analysis, outside administrative law's reach. What this conclusion misses is that VFS is a mandatory gateway to a statutory right — there is no way to submit a study permit application without going through VFS for biometrics. An error at that stage can delay or derail an application with serious downstream consequences for immigration status, employment authorization, and study continuity. The person harmed has no direct administrative law remedy against the body that caused the harm.
The source-of-power test was developed when government acted through its own officials, not through contracted private infrastructure. The Roncarelli principle — that public authority creates public accountability — has not yet been applied to ask who is accountable when public authority is intentionally routed through private architecture. Until the doctrine catches up with the reality of outsourced service delivery, there is a genuine accountability gap that affects people who have no way to avoid it.
Cases referenced: Roncarelli v Duplessis, [1959] SCR 121
